text: The carbon dioxide reduction to methane is highly desired for production of clean energy. Although transition metals such as Cu have been widely used for the catalytic reduction of $\text{CO}_2$, their catalytic performance remains unsatisfactory, which is largely ascribed to a lack of quantitative knowledge of how the catalyst structures and reaction networks can be rationally tailored to achieve selective and efficient reduction of $\text{CO}_2$ into methane. Here, the $\text{CO}_2$ reduction into methane at a high current efficiency using a dual-strategy design of the carbon-supported Cu catalysts is realized. In situ X-ray absorption and Raman spectroscopy reveal the reconstruction of the $\text{Cu-N}_3$ active sites into $\text{N,OH-Cu}_3$ clusters while performing the $\text{CO}_2$ reduction. The binding energies of *CO to the catalytically active sites and the energy barriers for the $\text{CO}$ hydrogenation over the prepared catalysts are significantly lowered than those over the metallic Cu surface and Cu-N3 active sites, suggesting a higher selectivity toward $\text{CO}_2$ reduction to $\text{CH}_4$ than the competitive and highly enthalpy-change-demanding $\text{CO}$ dimerization into $\text{C}_2$ species. It is remarkable that the structure of the carbon supports also has a significant influence on the catalytic performances, and using the block copolymer-derived carbon fiber with the desired morphology of a network of intertwined bicontinuous mesochannels, which endowed the $\text{CO}_2$ reduction into $\text{CH}_4$ with a suppressive $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ and prolonging $\text{CO}$ residence time, thus yields a high Faradaic efficiency of 74.2% at a relatively high current density of 300 mA $\text{cm}^{-2}$. Overall, this demonstration not only realizes the highly efficient reduction of $\text{CO}_2$ into $\text{CH}_4$ at high current densities but also provided quantitative knowledge regarding how the catalyst structures and reaction networks can be rationally tailored to achieve selective and efficient reduction of $\text{CO}_2$ through an atom-scale site engineering strategy coupled with mesoscale structural engineering approach.
